items.title: London Bread Trade Act 1815
items.summary: The London Bread Trade Act 1815 was a local act regulating the baking and sale of bread in London, including controls on weights, prices (tied to flour costs), ingredients (prohibiting adulteration), baking standards, and sales practices to prevent fraud and ensure 'fair' trade.
items.reason: This act exemplifies harmful interventionism: price and quality controls distort market signals, leading to unseen consequences like baker shortages (as profits vanish when flour prices rise), reduced supply, incentives for black markets or evasion, and stifled innovation in baking. Competition and private reputation would better ensure quality and fair pricing without central mandates, aligning with spontaneous order; retaining it burdens modern enterprise with obsolete rules, even if partially unenforced, preventing full liberty in food production.

items.title: Stamp Act 1815
items.summary: The Stamp Act 1815 repeals prior stamp duties on deeds, law proceedings, fire insurances, pawnbrokers' licenses, and legacies/successions, replacing them with new consolidated stamp duties on a wide array of legal instruments (e.g., admissions, agreements, bonds, bills/promissory notes, conveyances, leases, mortgages), insurances, probate/confirmations/inventories, legacies/residues, and court proceedings, managed by Commissioners of Stamps with penalties for evasion/forgery.
items.reason: Retaining this act imposes transaction taxes that distort incentives across commerce, property, finance, insurance, and probate: higher costs reduce volume of bills/notes/credit (hampering trade/banking), conveyances/mortgages (stifling property markets/investment), insurances (raising risk premiums/supply shortages), and proceedings/probate (burdening justice/inheritance). Unseen consequences include capital flight, black markets, reduced economic mobility, wealth creation suppressed as resources shift to avoidance; poor hit hardest via inflated costs/goods/services without targeted benefits, violating liberty/property foundations of prosperity.

items.title: Jury Trials (Scotland) Act 1815
items.summary: The Jury Trials (Scotland) Act 1815 introduced trial by jury for factual issues in civil cases referred from the Court of Session, detailing procedures for jury selection, trials, new trials, exceptions, oaths, fines, views, and related processes to extend jury trials to civil causes in Scotland.
items.reason: This Act is largely obsolete, with most sections repealed by later legislation including the Court of Session Act 1988 (ss. 1-19), Juries Act 1949, Statute Law Revision Act 1873, and others; remnants create statutory clutter, increasing unseen costs in legal research, interpretation risks, and maintenance without providing active benefits, as modern court rules govern civil jury procedures effectively, distorting no incentives but burdening the legal system unnecessarily.
